coredata.dc:title: Combustion synthesis of tantalum carbides TaC and Ta2C
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yeh, C.L.
Liu, E.W.
coredata.dc:description: The preparation of tantalum monocarbide TaC and hemicarbide Ta2C was conducted by self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) from elemental powder compacts in this study. Effects of starting stoichiometry, initial sample density, preheating temperature, and dilution by the final product on the combustion characteristics, as well as on the product composition were studied. Experimental observations indicate that with the progression of self-sustained combustion wave, the compact with a molar ratio of Ta:C=1:1 experiences substantial structure delamination, leading to considerable elongation of the burned sample. In contrast, the powder compact made up of Ta:C=2:1 almost retained its original shape after combustion. The flame-front propagation velocity was found to increase with initial sample density and temperature, but to decrease with diluent content. For both types of carbides, formation of the single-phase product was improved by increasing the sample green density. Complete conversion yielding TaC and Ta2C from the compacted samples with an initial density of 45% TMD was verified by the XRD analysis of end products. In addition, raising the sample temperature by prior heating was also proved to be effective in the enhancement of phase conversion. Based upon the dependence of flame-front velocity on combustion temperature, activation energies of 187.4 and 299.3kJ/mol were deduced for the formation of TaC and Ta2C by SHS, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tantalum carbide
TaC
Ta2C
Combustion synthesis
SHS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recorded combustion images associated with formation of TaC from a 45% TMD sample with starting stoichiometry Ta:C=1:1 and T p =100°C.
Recorded combustion images associated with formation of Ta2C from a 35% TMD sample with starting stoichiometry Ta:C=2:1.
Photographs of burned samples composed of tantalum carbides: (a) TaC and (b) Ta2C.
Typical temperature profile illustrating SHS process of Ta2C formation.
Typical temperature profile illustrating SHS process of TaC formation.
Effects of preheating temperature and initial sample density on flame-front propagation velocity of samples prepared for TaC synthesis.
Effect of initial sample density on flame-front propagation velocity of samples prepared for Ta2C synthesis.
Effect of diluent content on flame-front propagation velocity of samples in synthesis of TaC and Ta2C.
Relation between flame-front velocity and combustion temperature for determination of activation energies of TaC and Ta2C by SHS.
XRD spectra of burned products obtained from Ta:C=1:1 compacts with (a) 30% TMD and T p =100°C, (b) 30% TMD and T p =200°C, and (c) 45% TMD and T p =25°C.
XRD spectra of burned products obtained from Ta:C=2:1 compacts with (a) 30% TMD and (b) 45% TMD.

article.ce:title: Combustion synthesis of tantalum carbides TaC and Ta2C

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering, Da-Yeh University, 112 Shan-Jiau Rd., Da-Tsuen, Changhua 51505, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation of tantalum monocarbide TaC and hemicarbide Ta2C was conducted by self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) from elemental powder compacts in this study. Effects of starting stoichiometry, initial sample density, preheating temperature, and dilution by the final product on the combustion characteristics, as well as on the product composition were studied. Experimental observations indicate that with the progression of self-sustained combustion wave, the compact with a molar ratio of Ta:C=1:1 experiences substantial structure delamination, leading to considerable elongation of the burned sample. In contrast, the powder compact made up of Ta:C=2:1 almost retained its original shape after combustion. The flame-front propagation velocity was found to increase with initial sample density and temperature, but to decrease with diluent content. For both types of carbides, formation of the single-phase product was improved by increasing the sample green density. Complete conversion yielding TaC and Ta2C from the compacted samples with an initial density of 45% TMD was verified by the XRD analysis of end products. In addition, raising the sample temperature by prior heating was also proved to be effective in the enhancement of phase conversion. Based upon the dependence of flame-front velocity on combustion temperature, activation energies of 187.4 and 299.3kJ/mol were deduced for the formation of TaC and Ta2C by SHS, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Refractory metal carbides are promising ceramic materials, because these compounds exhibit unusual combinations of physical and chemical properties such as high hardness and high melting point (typical for semiconductors with covalent bonds) and good electrical and thermal conductivities (typical for compounds with metallic bonds) [1]. Additionally, many carbides of transition metals of the IVb and Vb groups crystallize in the NaCl structure, which is common in ionic compounds. Therefore, the chemical bond in those transition metal carbides is a superposition of covalent, metallic, and ionic components [1].

Tantalum monocarbide TaC has a NaCl type structure and is characterized by high hardness, high melting point (3880°C), great electrical conductivity (42.1μΩcm at 25°C), good resistance to chemical attack and thermal shock, and excellent resistance to oxidation [2]. These properties make it very attractive for high-temperature and electronic applications. The phase diagram of the Ta–C system shows another well-defined carbide, the hemicarbide Ta2C. Ta2C has a structure based upon a hexagonal closest-packed metal lattice with carbon atoms filling one half of the octahedral holes [3]. Tantalum hemicarbide is also featured by a high melting point of 3327°C. In addition, the hemicarbide Ta2C was found to possess a higher oxidation resistance in comparison to the monocarbide TaC [4].

Transition metal carbides, especially titanium carbide (TiC), were successfully produced by means of combustion synthesis from elemental powder compacts [5,6]. Combustion synthesis with the advantages of time and energy savings has been recognized as an attractive alternative to the conventional methods of producing advanced materials, including carbides, borides, nitrides, hydrides, silicides, intermetallics, etc. [6–10]. Furthermore, combustion synthesis can be conducted in either of two modes, the self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) mode and the thermal explosion mode. Regarding the formation of tantalum carbides by combustion synthesis, a survey of literature indicates that existing studies focus mainly on the production of monocarbide TaC, but rarely on the hemicarbide Ta2C. Shkiro et al. [11] conducted experiments to prepare TaC by SHS from the sample compacts made up of Ta and carbon black powders, observing an unsteady propagation of combustion wave and an incomplete conversion with 12% of Ta powders left unreacted. Knyazik and Shteinberg [12] attempted to produce TaC by thermal explosion synthesis and found the presence of not only TaC but also Ta2C in the final product. With the activation of an imposed electric field across the pellet, steady propagation of self-sustaining combustion wave and formation of the single-phase TaC in the end product were achieved by Xue and Munir [13]. In addition, Xue and Munir [13] proposed a solid diffusion mechanism including diffusion of carbon into the tantalum matrix, resulting in the formation of a TaC layer encapsulating a tantalum core. Subsequent conversion to TaC is governed by the carbon diffusion through the TaC layer, which is considered as the rate-limiting step. To enhance the solid-phase diffusion in the interaction between Ta and C, Kim and Wooldridge [14,15] performed catalytically assisted combustion synthesis with the use of vaporized iodine and carbon dioxide as the gas-phase transport agents that were found to substantially increase the burning velocity and to greatly improve the conversion efficiency.

The objective of the present study was to experimentally investigate the formation of tantalum carbides TaC and Ta2C by SHS using compacted samples from elemental powders. Effects of the sample green density, initial sample temperature, and starting composition on the combustion characteristics, as well as on the final composition of burned products were studied. The flame-front propagation velocity and combustion temperature were measured as a function of the experimental variables. On the basis of the dependence of flame-front velocity on combustion wave temperature, which was determined by diluting the green compact with its final product, the activation energies associated with the formation of TaC and Ta2C by SHS were deduced in this study.

Test samples used in this study were prepared by tantalum (Aldrich Chemical, −325 mesh, 99.9% purity) and carbon black (Showa Chemical Co., 22nm) powders, which were dry mixed in a ball mill for 10h with the molar compositions according to stoichiometric ratios of Ta:C=1:1 and 2:1 for the synthesis of TaC and Ta2C, respectively. Mixed powders were then cold-pressed into cylindrical samples with a diameter of 7mm and a height of 12mm. In order to study the influence of sample green density on the synthesis of tantalum carbides, sample compacts were formed with initial packing densities ranging from 30 to 45% of the theoretical maximum density (TMD) of reactant mixtures. The effect of initial sample temperature was studied by heating up the sample compact prior to ignition and the preheating temperatures (T p) adopted in this study were 100 and 200°C.

The SHS experiments were conducted in a stainless-steel windowed combustion chamber under an atmosphere of high purity argon (99.99%). The sample holder was equipped with a 600W cartridge heater used to raise the initial temperature of test samples prior to ignition. The experimental setup and measurement approach were previously reported, which have been used to synthesize many transition metal nitrides and carbonitrides [16–18] and some intermetallics [19–21]. However, it was found that when the reaction was initiated by a heated tungsten coil with a voltage of 60V and a current of 1.5A, the combustion wave could not be well established and finally came to extinction. Therefore, a compacted pellet (2mm in height and 7mm in diameter) made up of the mixture of titanium (Ti) and carbon black (C) powders (with a ratio of Ti:C=1:1) was placed on the top of the test sample to serve as the ignition enhancer in this study.

Experimental observations of this study indicate that the combustion behavior of Ta–C powder compacts varies drastically with the starting stoichiometry of test samples. Fig. 1 is representative of a typical sequence of the SHS process recorded from a compacted reactant with an initial composition of Ta:C=1:1 and a preheating temperature of 100°C. As shown in Fig. 1, upon ignition a distinct and glaring combustion front forms and travels along the sample in a self-sustaining manner. With the advance of combustion wave, significant delamination of the burned sample was clearly observed, which resulted in serious structure fracturing and substantial elongation on the end product.

In the experiment of producing Ta2C, a typical combustion process is illustrated in Fig. 2 , within which the reaction of a sample composed of 2Ta+C was initiated without any preheating. In contrast to those observed in Fig. 1, neither structure cracking nor volume expansion on the burned sample is noticed in Fig. 2. Fig. 2 shows that the combustion process is characterized by a self-propagating combustion front steadily spreading throughout the entire sample, and the compacted reactant essentially retains its original shape after the conversion to the final product. In addition to the fine exterior on the burned sample, Fig. 2 indicates that the compact exhibits a distinct afterburning glow. That is, after the flame front reaches the bottom of the sample at about t =0.63s, the luminosity on the burned sample fades away obviously, but from t =1.2s the brightness turns stronger and remains intense for a duration even longer than the flame-propagation period. This implies that the phase transformation is not only restricted to the combustion front, but also continues lengthily in bulk after the passage of the flame front. Based upon the observations of combustion processes, it is evident that the afterburning reaction in the sample of Ta:C=1:1 (shown in Fig. 1) is not as pronounced as that of Ta:C=2:1 (shown in Fig. 2). It is believed that the lack of extensive afterburning reaction in the compact of Ta:C=1:1 could be due to a greater degree of heat loss caused by the crevices formed on the burned sample.

Two burned samples recovered from the experiments synthesizing TaC and Ta2C are photographically displayed in Fig. 3 (a and b), respectively. The as-synthesized TaC pellet features numerous crevices and is noticeably taller than the Ta2C specimen, which exhibits a relatively smooth and shining exterior. In spite of striking difference in the outer appearance, the gold brown color of tantalum carbides is clearly seen on both burned samples.

A typical temperature profile measured on the surface of a sample with Ta:C=2:1 is presented in Fig. 4 . As depicted in Fig. 4, an abrupt rise in temperature signifies the rapid arrival of combustion front and the peak value corresponds to the flame-front temperature. After the passage of the flame front, the profile shows a sudden temperature drop, suggesting that the sample experiences a significant heat loss to the ambient. However, due to the occurrence of afterburning reaction, the decrease of sample temperature noticeably slows down following a short period of rapid decline. It is useful to note that the variation of sample temperature in the course of combustion is consistent with the observation of combustion images shown in Fig. 2.

In the experiment of forming the monocarbide TaC, the typical temperature profile shown in Fig. 5 reveals a sharp peak similar to that in Fig. 4, and a rapid decline after reaching the maximum value. A comparison of temperature profiles shown in Figs. 4 and 5 indicates that after the passage of combustion front, the decrease of temperature on the compact for the formation of TaC is more severe than that on the sample for Ta2C synthesis. This could be most likely attributed to the less pronounced afterburning reaction taking place in the sample of Ta:C=1:1. Otherwise, this might be due to the cracking of the sample, resulting in the dislocation of the thermocouple which was originally attached on the sample surface.

The variation of flame-front propagation velocity (V f) with sample green density and preheating temperature is plotted in Fig. 6 for the synthesis of monocarbide TaC. It was found that the flame-front velocity was increased by increasing the initial sample density. This is caused by the fact that the sample with a higher compact density corresponds to the possession of a better degree of contact between reactant powders, which in turn enhances the heat transfer rate from the combustion front to the unburned region. Additionally, the flame velocity also increased with preheating temperature, primarily due to the increase of combustion temperature with initial sample temperature. However, it should be noted that for the 30% TMD sample composed of Ta:C=1:1, the reaction was quenched under the condition without any prior heating and the self-sustained combustion was realized only when the compact was heated up to 100°C or above before ignition.

On the other hand, experimental results show the preheating is not required on the powder compacts made up of Ta:C=2:1. As a result, the velocity of self-propagating combustion wave in the synthesis of Ta2C was measured under different initial sample densities, as shown in Fig. 7 . In agreement with the result presented in Fig. 6, Fig. 7 indicates the increase of flame-front velocity with sample green density. Moreover, it was found that within the test conditions of this study the flame-front velocities associated with the synthesis of Ta2C (ranging from 14 to 18.6mm/s) were higher than those measured from the compacts prepared for the formation of TaC (between 7.5 and 10.5mm/s) reported in Fig. 6.

While the green compacts are diluted by their final products, Fig. 8 points out that both types of samples (including Ta+C and 2Ta+C) reveal a decrease in flame-front velocity as the amount of diluent increases. This is mainly because of the decrease of combustion temperature with increasing content of diluent in the green compact. Moreover, measured results show a distinct dependence of reaction front velocity on combustion temperature, which provides a basis for the determination of activation energy associated with the formation of tantalum carbides by SHS [19–21]. A relationship correlating combustion temperature, flame velocity, and activation energy is expressed in the following simplified form [19–21]
(1)
V f
T c
2
= f ( n )
R
E a
K
exp
−
E a
R
T c
where V f is the velocity of combustion front, T c is the combustion temperature, E a is the activation energy of the reaction, R is the universal gas constant, f(n) is a function of the kinetic order of the reaction, and K is a constant which includes the heat capacity of the product, thermal conductivity, and the heat of reaction. As a result, the slope of a plot depicting ln(V f/T c)2 versus 1/T c can provide the activation energy of the process. From the slopes of best-fit lines of all the data plotted in Fig. 9 , the deduced activation energies for combustion synthesis of TaC and Ta2C were found to be 187.4 and 299.3kJ/mol, respectively.

The XRD spectra obtained from the powder compacts with an initial composition of Ta:C=1:1 under different test conditions are presented in Fig. 10 . Fig. 10(a) reveals the formation of monocarbide TaC and the existence of a significant amount of elemental Ta unreacted. This result shows evidence of incomplete conversion in a 30% TMD sample under a preheating temperature of 100°C. However, the phase transformation was found to be effectively improved by increasing the initial sample temperature. As shown in Fig. 10(b), under the case of T p =200°C the end product is composed of a single-phase constituent TaC. In addition, the improvement of phase conversion to the fully reacted product was also achieved by increasing the sample green density. This is confirmed by Fig. 10(c), which indicates the formation of stoichiometric TaC from a 45% TMD sample compact without any heating prior to ignition.

In the case of producing hemicarbide Ta2C, Fig. 11 (a) shows that in addition to the dominant phase Ta2C there exists a small amount of monocarbide TaC being the intermediate phase in the burned product from a 30% TMD sample. Consistent with the finding observed in the synthesis of TaC, the conversion to Ta2C was enhanced by increasing the sample green density. As shown in Fig. 11(b), an end product composed entirely of the hemicarbide Ta2C was obtained from the sample with a green density of 45% TMD.

This study presents a comprehensive investigation of the SHS process associated with the formation of tantalum monocarbide TaC and hemicarbide Ta2C. It was found that the combustion behavior varied with the starting stoichiometry of sample compacts, and the final composition of burned products was affected by the experimental parameters, including the sample green density and preheating temperature.

Experimental observations indicate that self-sustained combustion was achieved on both types of samples with Ta:C=1:1 and 2:1. As the combustion wave advanced, the compact with an initial stoichiometry Ta:C=1:1 cracked seriously, leading to considerable elongation of the burned sample. On the contrary, the sample of Ta:C=2:1 basically retained its original shape after conversion to the final product. Moreover, subsequent to the passage of the flame front the sample of Ta:C=2:1 exhibited a more pronounced afterburning stage than did the compact of Ta:C=1:1, which was subjected to a greater heat loss due to the structure delamination.

In the synthesis of either of two carbides, the flame-front propagation velocity was found to increase with sample green density. The increase of sample green density not only results in a higher flame-front velocity, but also effectively improves the phase transformation. On the formation of TaC, the product obtained from a 30% TMD compact at a preheating temperature of 100°C consists of a significant amount of unreacted Ta. However, complete conversion to a fully reacted product TaC was achieved on a compact of 45% TMD. Likewise, in addition to the dominant phase Ta2C the presence of TaC was found as the intermediate phase in the final product of a 30% TMD compact with Ta:C=2:1, suggesting the incompleteness of phase conversion. By using the sample with a higher density such as 45% TMD, however, the interaction was improved and the formation of a single-phase product Ta2C was identified by the XRD analysis.

The influence of initial sample temperature was only investigated on the synthesis of monocarbide TaC, since the self-sustained combustion cannot be established on the low-density compact of Ta:C=1:1 without heating up the sample prior to ignition. The increase of sample temperature by preheating was demonstrated to increase the flame-front velocity, as well as to enhance the formation of stoichiometric product TaC.

The dependence of flame-front velocity on combustion temperature was attained in this study by diluting the green compacts with their final products. As a consequence, the activation energies associated with combustion synthesis of TaC and Ta2C were determined to be 187.4 and 299.3kJ/mol, respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was sponsored by the National Science Council of Taiwan, ROC, under the grants of NSC 92-2212-E-212-015 and NSC 93-2212-E-212-002.
